concepts.title: Shearing Stress and Strain
concepts.conceptBody:
Shearing Stress and Strain

 

If the deforming force tries to change the shape of a body, a shear stress is developed in the body. In the diagram, we see a force $\text{F}$ parallel to the surface $\text{ABCD}$ of area $\text{A}$, deforms the rectangular area $ABPQ$ into a parallelogram.

[Let $\left. \text{A}{{\text{A}}^{'}}=\text{Dx}~\And~ \text{AQ}=\text{y} \right]$

Hence due to $\text{F}$, the shape of the body is altered.

shear stress $~=\frac{~\text{ shearing }~\text{ force }~}{~\text{ cross }~\text{ section }~\text{ of }~\text{ ABCD}}$

$=\frac{\text{F}}{\text{A}}$

 

shear strain is defined as:

shear strain $~=\frac{\Delta x}{y}=\text{tan}\theta =\theta (\theta$ is very small)

concepts.title: Modulus of Rigidity
concepts.conceptBody:
Modulus of Rigidity ( $\eta $ ) 

Within limits of proportionality, the ratio of tangential stress to the shearing strain is called modulus of rigidity of the material of the body and is denoted by $\eta $, i.e.,

$\eta =\frac{\text{ Shearing }\text{ stress }}{\text{ Shearing }\text{ strain }}$

In this case the shape of a body changes but its volume remains unchanged.

Consider a cube of material fixed at its lower face and acted upon by a tangential force $\text{F}$ at its upper surface having area A. The shearing stress, then, will be

$\text{ Shearing }\text{ stress }=\frac{{{\text{F}}_{\parallel }}}{\text{A}}=\frac{\text{F}}{\text{A}}$

This shearing force causes the consecutive horizontal layers of the cube to be slightly displaced or sheared relative to one another : each line such as AB or CD in the cube is rotated through an angle $\phi $ by this shear. The shearing strain is defined as the angle $\phi $ in radians through which a line normal to a fixed surface has turned. For small values of angle,

$\text{ Shearing }\text{ strain }=\phi =\frac{\text{A}{{\text{A}}^{'}}}{\text{AB}}=\frac{\text{x}}{\text{L}}$

so $\eta =\frac{\text{ shear }\text{ stress }}{\text{ shear }\text{ strain }}=\frac{\text{F}/\text{A}}{\phi }=\frac{\text{F}}{\text{A}\phi }$

Only solids can exhibit a shearing as these have definite shape.

Note: Poisson discovered that within limits of proportionality the ratio of the lateral strain to longitudinal strain is constant for a given material. This constant in his honour is called Poisson's ratio and is represented by $\sigma $. It has no units and dimensions. It has been established that theoretically $-1<\sigma <\left( 1/2 \right)$ while practically no substance has been found for which $\sigma $ is negative, i.e., practically $0<\sigma <\left( 1/2 \right)$.

Regarding moduli of elasticity $(\text{Y},\beta $ and $\eta $ ) it is worthy to note that :

(1) The value of moduli of elasticity is independent of the magnitude of the stress and strain. It depends only on the nature of the material of the body.

(2) For a given material there can be different moduli of elasticity depending on the type of stress applied and the strain resulting.

(3) The moduli of elasticity has same dimensional formula and units as that of stress since strain is dimensionless, i.e., the dimensional formula for $\text{Y},\text{B}$ or $\eta $ is $]\text{M}{{\text{L}}^{-1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{T}}^{-2}}$ ] while units dyne/ $\text{c}{{\text{m}}^{2}}$ or newton $/{{\text{m}}^{2}}$.

(4) Greater the value of moduli of elasticity, more elastic is the material. But as $\text{Y}\propto \left( 1/\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ L} \right)$, $B\propto \left( 1/\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ }V \right)$ and $\eta \propto \left( 1/\phi \right)$ for a constant stress, so smaller change of shape or size for a given stress corresponds to greater elasticity.

(5) The moduli of elasticity $\text{Y}$ and $\eta $ exist only for solids as liquids and gases cannot be deformed along one dimension only and also cannot sustain shear strain. However, $\beta $ exists for all states of matter, viz., solid, liquid or gas.

(6) Gases being most compressible are least elastic while solids are most, i.e., the bulk modulus of gases is very low while that for liquids and solids is very high, i.e.,

${{\text{E}}_{\text{solid }}}>{{\text{E}}_{\text{liquid }}}>{{\text{E}}_{\text{gas }}}$

$\therefore \eta ($ Modulus of Rigidity)

$=\frac{\text{ Tangential }\text{ stress }}{\text{ shearing }\text{ strain }}=\frac{\text{F}/\text{a}}{\frac{\Delta\text{ L}}{\text{L}}}=\frac{\text{F}}{\text{a}}\times \frac{\text{L}}{\Delta\text{ L}}=\frac{\text{F}}{\theta \cdot \text{A}}$

Relation between $Y,K$ and $\eta $

$\frac{3}{\text{Y}}=\frac{1}{\eta }+\frac{1}{3 \text{ K}}$
